did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes and evaluates 'Skin-Net', a novel deep residual network (Residual Deep Convolution Neural Network) for the classification of skin lesions. The method utilizes multilevel feature extraction and cross-channel correlation to classify benign and malignant skin lesions using the ISIC-2019 and ISIC-2020 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents 'Skin-Net', a novel deep residual network for skin lesion classification. It details a specific methodology involving multilevel feature extraction and cross-channel correlation, describes experimental setups using ISIC-2019 and ISIC-2020 datasets, and reports original quantitative results (e.g., accuracy, sensitivity) comparing the proposed model against existing approaches. The structure includes Introduction, Methodology, Experimental Results, and Conclusion, indicating original experimental research rather than a literature review.

title: Skin‑Net: a novel deep residual network for skin lesions classification using multilevel feature extraction and cross‑channel correlation with detection of outlier
brief_summary: This study proposes Skin-Net, a novel residual deep convolutional neural network designed to classify skin lesions by leveraging multilevel feature extraction and cross-channel correlation. The method addresses dataset imbalance by converting images and labels into vectors of images and weights, and it utilizes sliding dot product filters for feature extraction. The model was evaluated on the challenging ISIC-2019 and ISIC-2020 datasets for multiclass classification tasks. Results indicate that Skin-Net outperformed existing deep convolutional networks and state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, precision, and F-score. Specifically, the proposed method achieved an accuracy of 98.69% on the ISIC-2020 dataset. The findings demonstrate the reliability and effectiveness of the proposed architecture for automated skin lesion diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (benign vs malignant binary classification and multiclass classification of 7 specific lesion types including melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes Skin-Net for the classification of skin lesions. It performs binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and multiclass classification (7 classes on ISIC-2020 and 9 classes on ISIC-2019). The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories using a Residual Deep Convolution Neural Network. Performance is measured using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F-score.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A challenge dataset from the ISIC archive, composed of ISIC-2018 (HAM10000) and BCN_20000. HAM10000 images are 600x450 pixels, and BCN_20000 images are 1024x1024 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the Skin-Net model.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; used in the context of the challenge dataset.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation of the proposed method.
use.other: Compared against state-of-the-art methods in Table 7.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2020 (2020 SIIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Challenge)
datasets_used.description: A dataset hosted on Kaggle created by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It contains dermoscopic training images from over 2000 patients. Images were provided by Hospital Clínic de Barcelona, Medical University of Vienna, Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Melanoma Institute Australia, and University of Queensland University of Athens Medical School. Malignant diagnoses were confirmed by histopathology; benign diagnoses by expert agreement, longitudinal follow-up, and histopathology.

use.training: Used for training the Skin-Net model.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; used in the context of the challenge dataset.
use.testing: Used for testing and evaluation of the proposed method.
use.other: Compared against state-of-the-art methods in Table 8.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F-score, with comparisons made based on these metric values against state-of-the-art methods.

ISIC-2019.augmentation: Applied to classes with < 1000 images (AK, DF, SCC, Unknown, VASC)
ISIC-2019.notes: The proposed method achieved the highest values for all measures compared with methods [56, 59]. Method [59] merged classes into malignant and benign.

ISIC-2020.augmentation: None applied to original dataset distribution
ISIC-2020.notes: The proposed method obtained the highest values for accuracy, specificity, and precision compared with methods [26, 57, 58]. All compared methods used binary classification, whereas the proposed method used multiclass (7 classes).

architecture_used.main_model: Skin-Net (Residual Deep Convolution Neural Network / RDCNN)
architecture_used.input_size: 300 x 300 x 3 (Height x Width x Channels)

key_components: Multilevel feature extraction using 3x3 and 1x1 local concatenated filters
Cross-channel correlation (ignoring spatial dimensions using 1x1 convolution through residual block)
architecture_details.feature_extraction_method: Sliding dot product filters for multi-layer feature extraction and cross-channel correlation

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (SoftMax layer implies Cross-Entropy)

hyperparameters.learning_rate_scheduling: Drop learning rate if loss does not enhance after 10 epochs

training_details.augmentation: Applied only to classes with < 1000 images (ISIC-2019); None applied to ISIC-2020 original distribution
training_details.hardware: IBM PC (Core i7, 16 GB DDRAM), NVIDIA MX150 GPU

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer followed by SoftMax

Method [26] (Fuzzy C-means and Red Fox Optimization)

preprocessing_techniques: No preprocessing steps were applied to the images by the proposed Skin-Net method.
Data imbalance handling via conversion of dataset from image and label to vector of image and weight.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with angles ranging from 0 to 360 degrees.
Applied only to classes with fewer than 1000 images in the ISIC-2019 dataset (specifically AK, DF, SCC, Unknown, and VASC). No augmentation was applied to the ISIC-2020 dataset.

conclusions: The proposed Skin-Net model, a novel deep residual network with 54 layers, effectively classifies skin lesions by utilizing multilevel feature extraction (using 3x3 and 1x1 filters) and cross-channel correlation while ignoring spatial dimensions.
The method addresses dataset imbalance by converting the dataset from image and label format to a vector of image and weight, preventing bias toward majority classes.
Skin-Net outperformed existing deep convolutional networks and state-of-the-art methods on the ISIC-2019 and ISIC-2020 datasets in terms of accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, precision, and F-score.
On the ISIC-2019 dataset, Skin-Net achieved an accuracy of 94.65%, specificity of 96.78%, sensitivity of 70.78%, precision of 72.56%, and F-score of 71.33%.
On the ISIC-2020 dataset, Skin-Net achieved an accuracy of 98.69%, specificity of 99.28%, sensitivity of 95.43%, precision of 95.43%, and F-score of 93.79%.
The proposed method does not require pre-processing steps such as hair removal, enhancement, or segmentation, unlike some compared methods.
The model is not lightweight and cannot operate on devices with limited memory or microdevices due to high running time and resource requirements.
The proposed method was tested only for skin lesions and requires further generalization for different classification tasks and identification of various malignancies.
important_remarks: The model is not lightweight and cannot operate on devices with limited memory or microdevices due to high running time and resource requirements.
The proposed method was tested only for skin lesions and requires further generalization for different classification tasks and identification of various malignancies.

The method addresses dataset imbalance by converting the dataset from image and label to a vector of image and weight, which may lead to weight changes if dataset images change, potentially requiring weight reproduction.
No formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests were reported; performance comparisons were based on metric values against state-of-the-art methods.
The ISIC-2020 dataset contains a highly imbalanced class distribution, with 'Unknown' comprising 27,126 out of 33,126 images, while classes like 'Solar lentigo' have only 7 images.
Data augmentation was applied only to classes with fewer than 1000 images in ISIC-2019; no augmentation was applied to the ISIC-2020 dataset.
The proposed method does not require pre-processing steps such as hair removal, enhancement, or segmentation, unlike some compared methods.
The study lacks external validation beyond the ISIC-2019 and ISIC-2020 datasets, limiting the assessment of generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
